source_metadata.title_raw: Karl H. Berger - A-18-108 - June 12, 2018 - Burbank

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Karl H. Berger 
Bellflower City Attorney 
Hensley Law Group,-Burbank Office 
2600 W. Olive Avenue, Suite 500 
Burbank, CA 91505 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-108 
Dear Mr. Berger: 
June 12, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Bellflower Mayor Raymond 
Dunton and City Councilmember Ron Schnabl egger regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Pl·ease note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest 
prohibitions, such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) l 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May Mayor Dunton and Councilmember Schnabblegger take part in decisions regarding the 
potential development of a property and purchase of an adjacent property located within 500 feet of 
their respective financial interests? 
CONCLUSION 
Mayor Dunton and Councilmember Schnabblegger have disqualifying conflicts of interest 
in the decisions related to the development of the property and purchase of an adjacent property. 
However, given that Councilmember Juan Garza has also recused himself from decisions related to 
the development project, the Council may invoke the "legally required participation" exception to 
form a quorum of members with respect to the decisions, as specified in Regulation I 8705. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 JO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED ~y THE REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-108 
P~ge No. 2 
The City of Bellflower is located in Los Angeles County. The City is 6.1 square miles in 
size and, according to the 2016 United States ~ensus, has a population of77,790. The City Council 
is composed of five members, who are elected at large. 
The City is considering the possible conveyance of the City-owned property known as the 
Greek Market (the "Site"). The Site is located in a prominent location in the City, at the northern 
entrance to the City's downtown area. The Site is directly across the street from the Bellflower 
Event Center and the Los Angeles County Fire Museum. If revitalized, the Site would benefit the 
public by markedly enhancing the City's downtown area. 
For the past several months, developers have been contacting the City to propose various 
types of developments for the Site. City staff has narrowed the field of interested developers into 
four groups and has requested that each group provide information related to the proposed 
development (e.g. site plan, implementation plan, proof of neighborhood compatibility). Three of 
the four development groups submitted proposal letters to the City Council for its consideration. 
In conjunction with the recent interests to develop the Site, the City sought to explore the 
potential for developing the entire Site. The City Manager and the City's Economic Development 
Director, met with neighboring property owners to discuss the acquisition of additional properties at 
the Site. One property owner has expressed an interest in selling real property to the City (the 
"Property"). 
Mayor Raymond Dunton owns a multifamily rental property, which has a fair market value 
exceeding $2,000, within approximately 106 feet of the Site, and 53 feet from the Property. 
City Councilmember Ron Schnabblegger has a 17-percent beneficial interest in an autobody 
repair and paint shop, which is one of multiple properties owned as part of a family trust. The 
autobody shop lies 53 feet from the Site and 106 feet from the Property. While Councilmember 
Schnabblegger receives a check every three months for his share of trust income, he is not a 
managing partner of the trust, has no authority over the trust, and is not responsible for designating 
who will enjoy the properties or income related to the trust. The trust is irrevocable and has a value 
exceeding $2,000. 
Separately, Councilmember Juan Garza is Senior Vice President of Englander, Knabe & 
Allen ("EK.A"), a strategic communications firm specializing in government affairs, political 
campaigns, reputation and crisis communications, association management and litigation support. 
One of the three development groups, which has submitted a proposal to the City Council in 
connection with the Site, is reportedly a client of EK.A. Due to this relationship, Councilmember 
Garza has already declared a conflict and has chosen not to participate in the Council's 
consideration of either the Site or the Property. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-18-108 
Page No. 3 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 
18700(a).) Relevant to the provided facts, Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict 
of interest may arise, including: 
• 
An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of , 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• 
An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b ). ) 
• 
An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See 
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.2 
Regulation 18234(a) provides that an official has an interest in the pro rata share of the 
interest in real property, sources of income, and investments of a trust in which the official has a 
direct, indirect, or beneficial interest of I 0-percent or greater. 
Mayor Dunton 
Mayor Dunton owns a multifamily real property and utilizes the property as a rental 
enterprise, which qualifies as a .. business entity" under Section 82005. Under the provided facts, 
Mayor Dunton has interests in the property, the rental business, and sources of income from both 
the business and his tenants. 
Councilmember Sc/111abblegger 
Councilmember Schnabblegger has a 17-percent beneficial interest in an autobody and paint 
shop, and the property is owned by a family trust. Relevant to the provided facts, Councilmember 
Schnabblegger has interests in the business, assuming he has an investment of $2,000 or more, and 
the source of income received from the business, assuming he receives income aggregating $500 or 
more in the previous 12 months. 
2 An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family members is not 
considered separately if the decision also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation 
18702.S(c)). Because the decisions at issue also affect the Councilmembers' business and real property interests, we do 
not further analyze their personal financial interests. 


File No. A-18-108 
Page No. 4 
Additionally, you noted that Councilmember Schnabblegger receives payments from the 
trust and that the autobody shop is one of many properties owned by the family trust. Assuming 
Councilmember Schnabblegger has a 10-percent or greater interest in the trust, he also has an 
interest in the pro rata share of trust's interests in the property real property, sources of income, and 
investments of the trust. 
Foreseeability a11d Materiality 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for 
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
(Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party 
in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) Regulation 18701 (b) 
provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." Mayor Dunton's and Councilmember 
Schnabblegger's interests are not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue. 
Mayor Dunton 
Regulation l 8702.2(a)(7) through ( 12) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a 
decision's financial effect on an official's interest in real property that is not explicitly involved in 
the decision. In pertinent part, a financial effect is material if the decision: 
"(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than a 
commercial property containing a business entity .... Notwithstanding this 
prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to 
participate under these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are 
sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable 
impact on the official's property." 
In this case, Mayor Dunton owns a multifamily residential rental property and Regulation 
18702.2(a)(l l) will apply. Based upon the information provided, there are insufficient facts to 
determine that there will be no reasonably measurable impact on the official's property. Therefore, 
because Mayor Dunton's rental property is within 500 feet of the Site and the Property, the financial 
effect of the Council's decisions is material, and Mayor Dunton may not take part in the Council's 
decisions regarding the development of the Site and the purchase of the Property.3 
Councilmember Sclmabblegger 
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on 
an official's interest in a business. Regulation 18702. l (b) provides that a decision's effect is 
material if a prudent person with sufficient infonnation would find it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business entity. 
3 Because we conclude that Mayor Dunton has a conflict of interest based on his property interest, we do not 
analyze his other financial interests. 


File No. A-18-108 
Page No. 5 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides examples of decisions that may be applicable to detennine whether 
a decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business, including: 
"( 1} Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an 
activity in which the business is engaged; 
"(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the 
business entity is engaged; 
"(3). Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the 
business entity supplies; 
"(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as 
redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may 
affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity 
receives; 
"(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or 
other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws 
from the location of the project; or 
"(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability 
of the business entity." 
You stated that the Site and the Property are in a prominent location in the downtown area, 
and, if revitalized, the development would markedly enhance the area. Given the significance of the 
revitalization and the close proximity of the business, the project will likely have an impact on 
Councilmember Schnabblegger's business interest as revitalization of the area "may affect either 
temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the entity receives" and would "increase or 
decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the project . . .. " (Regulation 
18702.1 (b)(4)-(5).) Based on the considerable revitalization of the area, a prudent person with 
sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions' effects would 
contribute to a change in the value of Councilmember Schnabblegger's business. Therefore, 
Councilmember Schnabblegger has a disqualifying financial interest in decisions regarding the 
Site's future development, which includes the potential purchase of the Property.4 
Legally Required Participatio11 Exception 
Under the "legally required participation exception" set forth in Section 87101 and 
Regulation 18705, an official may make or participate in the making of a decision despite being 
financially interested in the decision if the official's participation is legally required for the action or 
decision to be made. However, Regulation 18705(c) requires the exception to be construed 
4 Because we conclude that Councilmember Schnabblegger has a conflict of interest based on his business 
interest, we do not analyze his other financial interests. 


File No. A-18-108 
Page No. 6 
narrowly. The exception cannot be invoked to allow a financially interested official to participate in 
a decision if a quorum can be convened of other members of the agency who are not disqualified. 5 
aecause the City Council is comprised of five members, at least three members are 
necessary to fonn a ·quorum to participate in decisions concerning the future development of the 
Site and the potential purchase of the Property. Mayor Dunton and Councilmember Schnabblegger 
have disqualifying conflicts of interest and Councilmember Garza has recused himself. 
Accordingly, the Council is authorized to use a random means of selection to select a single 
councilmember from among Mayor Dunton, Councilmember Schnabblegger, and Councilmember 
Garza to fonn a quorum with respect to the decisions at issue. (See Regulation 18705( c )(3 ). ) When 
one of the members is selected, that councilmember is selected for the duration of the proceedings 
in all related matters until that councilmember's participation is no longer legally required, or the 
need for relying on the exception no longer exists. (Ibid.) The selected councilmember will be 
required to state the existence of the potential conflict of interest as specified in Regulation 
18705(b). 
Accordingly, Mayor Dunton and Councilmember Schnabblegger have disqualifying 
conflicts of interest in decisions relating to the future development of the Site and the purchase of 
the Property. However, the Council may invoke "the legally required participation" exception to 
fonn a quorum with respect to those decisions, as specified in Regulation 18705. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
SKP:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Co 
Counsel, Legal Division 
5 Regulation 18705(d) defines a "quorum" as "the minimum number of members required to conduct 
business."

May Mayor Dunton and Councilmember Schnabblegger take part in decisions regarding the 
potential development of a property and purchase of an adjacent property located within 500 feet of 
their respective financial interests?

activity in which the business is engaged; 
"(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the

"(3). Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the

"(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as

QUESTION May Mayor Dunton and Councilmember Schnabblegger take part in decisions regarding the potential development of a property and purchase of an adjacent property located within 500 feet of their respective financial interests? CONCLUSION Mayor Dunton and Councilmember Schnabblegger have disqualifying conflicts of interest in the decisions related to the development of the property and purchase of an adjacent property. However, given that Councilmember Juan Garza has also recused himself from decisions related to the development project, the Council may invoke the "legally required participation" exception to form a quorum of members with respect to the decisions, as specified in Regulation I 8705. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 181 JO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS AS PROVIDED ~y THE REQUESTOR File No. A-18-108 P~ge No. 2 The City of Bellflower is located in Los Angeles County. The City is 6.1 square miles in size and, according to the 2016 United States ~ensus, has a population of77,790. The City Council is composed of five members, who are elected at large. The City is considering the possible conveyance of the City-owned property known as the Greek Market (the "Site"). The Site is located in a prominent location in the City, at the northern entrance to the City's downtown area. The Site is directly across the street from the Bellflower Event Center and the Los Angeles County Fire Museum. If revitalized, the Site would benefit the public by markedly enhancing the City's downtown area.
